descriptions.value: “A large grizzly was killed near Bear mountain a few days since, and but a short distance from San Andreas.” News of the Morning, Grizzly Killed, Sacramento Daily Union, 21 November 1859. Racialized content: engage this history with respect and care. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1859.1 for full context and citations. descriptions.value: “The presence of numerous grizzly bears near town is reported; the heavy fall of snow in the Sierra Nevadas has driven the deer, bear, and other large game down to the snowless region.” San Andreas, San Joaquin Republican, 20 November 1859. descriptions.value: “We clip the following items of news from the Plumas Standard of the 14th: Disagreeable Encounter. — As Mr. Shick, of this place, was returning from the O’Hara claims on the hill back of town, on yesterday, he encountered a large bear, which he thinks must have been a grizzly. When within about a mile of Quincy, Mr. S. Lost his way, and endeavoring to find the trail, he came across some tracks which he supposed would lead to it, following which he came to a clump of bushes from which emerged the bear. Rearing, at the sight of our hero, upon his hind legs, as if for a friendly greeting, whereupon Shick drew his pocket-knife, determined to make his best fight. Bruin, however, declined any further contest, and Shick, greatly relieved, sought tracks in a different direction. It is fortunate that he escaped so readily. Once the above was written, our “Nimrods” who, immediately started on receipt of the intelligence, after the bear, have returned, bringing with them, as a trophy, the dead carcass of the venerable bruin with two of his progeny who were alive and kicking, and we might add, scratching, also.” Plumas County Items, Marysville Daily Appeal, 20 April 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for the UANs GB.1860.5, GB.1860.5A, GB.1860.5B for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “A Bear Fight.— A party of gentlemen, from Antelope, were out hunting last week, when they fell in with an old bear and a cub—the latter weighing some two hundred pounds. Mr. Moore was attacked and pretty roughly handled by his cubship, and had not one of the party released his grip by a rifle ball, he would have breathed his last in young bruin’s embrace ; as it was, he came off with a badly mangled thumb and fingers, and a few minor wounds. Jeff Hickman crippled the old bear, but in the excitement of relieving their companion, and killing grizzly junior, grizzly senior sagaciously placed a safe distance between herself and danger, thus saving her hide and tallow for another occasion. Our informant says it was the roughest squabble he ever participated in.” Red Bluff Beacon, May 2 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1860.6 and GB.1860.6A for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “A party of three persons went out, on the 12th inst., in search of a grizzly. They found him, and fired several times, some of the shots taking effect, though not with desirable satisfaction. The bear, after being wounded, started after his tormenters, two of the hunters running for their dear lives, and the third, Joshua Herrington, remaining to give bruin another round. Herrington attempted to fire, but the cap on his gun snapped. He then endeavored to escape, but stumbled over a log, and was overtaken by the bear. In the encounter which followed, between Herrington and bruin, the former fared badly—receiving a severe wound on the head, another on the thigh, and a third on one of his hands. He escaped with these injuries, and is now recovering slowly. The day following, 13th, a party went out after the bear, but returned to town without accomplishing anything. A third was formed, started out, and saved the animal’s carcass. The grizzly was large, but being quite poor he weighed only five hundred pounds. One of his hind feet, which was measured, was eleven and a half inches in length and seven inches wide.” A Bear Story, Nevada Democrat, May 30 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.1 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “A grizzly bear was recently killed near Jamison City, Sierra co. It weighed five hundred pounds. A man named Herrington had a fight with it previously and was considerably injured.” A Grizzly Killed, Sacramento Daily Union, May 22 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.1 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “A few days previous to this adventure, a cinnamon bear was killed near Jamison. It is bearly possible that the whole story is a “sell” — we think not, however.” A Bear Story, Nevada Democrat, May 30 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for BB.1860.1 and GB.1860.1 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “We are informed by a gentleman from the mountains, that a hunter killed three grizzly bears in one week, lately, on the divide between the south and middle forks of Feather River, near the Mountain Spring House. The brutes weighed from 400 to 600 pounds each. The section of country named is situated in Butte County, about forty miles from Oroville. It is a very rugged and wild region, where new ravine, creek and hydraulic mines are being developed, and young settlements are springing up. It is accessible only by mule trail, at present.” Grizzly Hunting, Marysville Daily Appeal, June 5 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.2, GB.1860.3, and GB.1860.4 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “On Wednesday last two gentlemen of San Jose started out on a trout-fishing excursion in the hills east of that place, and in the course of two hours succeeded in bagging two hundred and thirty-six fine speckled trout. In addition to this good luck, one of the hombres was so fortunate as to be treed by a huge grizzly bear. The only result of the last named adventure, was a mutual fright of the bear and trout catcher.” Fishing Extraordinary, Daily National Democrat, June 10 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1860.2 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “A few days since, a large grizzly bear weighing 1,140 pounds, was killed on the ranch of Mr. J. S. Bracket, by a party of men. He had long been known to the ranchmen in that vicinity, and was called the “Big Bear of Marin.” Numerous attempts had been made to kill him. When first discovered by the party above mentioned, he rushed upon them, and crushed one of them to the earth, but before he had time to injure him severely, he was shot down by the others. Bracket’s Ranch Sonoma county.” Big Bear, Daily National Democrat, June 17 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.7 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “On the following day a party consisting of Mr Bracket, Jules Poirsson, T. H. Estey, S. Steadman, V. Bonnard, C. L. Estey and Mr. Cooper, took his trail, and had proceeded but a short distance when, upon entering the woods, the bear rushed upon them without warning, and making at Mons. Bonnard crushed him to the earth. — As the bear approached, Bonnard discharged his rifle but without effect. Before time had been given the bear for attacking the prostrate man, T.H. Estey planted a ball in his neck, which brought him to the earth, when he was speedily dispatched. Bonnard’s injuries, wonderful to state, consisted only of a slight flesh wound on the left arm.” The Big Bear of Marin Killed, Hydraulic Press, July 7 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.7 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “The bear was brought to this city on the following day, where he excited considerable interest. His weight was 1,140 pounds, which being altogether too strong for the people of this burg to stomach, his captors deemed it prudent to ship him to San Francisco, where the lovers of “bar meat” no doubt had an opportunity to gratify the taste to their full content.” The Big Bear of Marin Killed, Hydraulic Press, July 7 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.7 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “The contributions levied upon the stock-raisers of that county by his bear ship during the past four or five years has been immense, for with age also came wisdom and a sweet tooth, and during late years, a fresh bullock, calf, or a pig, has invariably been required at each and every repast in order to satisfy his appetite. In his early days, it is said, he not unfrequently returned to the dead carcass of a slaughtered bullock, for a second and even third repast, but age and experience had probably taught him the necessity of abandoning this practice, lest he in turn should fall a victim. Numerous and energetic have been the efforts made by the people of Marin to destroy this monster.” The Big Bear of Marin Killed, Hydraulic Press, July 7 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.7 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “Some three years since, we are told an effort was made by a party of natives who came upon him in an opening and succeeded in throwing a lasso about him. Probably regarding the attack as a bit of fun to see which of the two was the strongest, bruin planted himself upon his haunches, and seizing the lariat commenced gathering it in, at each haul bringing horse and rider so much nearer. The assailants finding how matters would probably terminate, cut loose.” The Big Bear of Marin Killed, Hydraulic Press, July 7 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.7 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “The contributions levied upon the stock-raisers of that county by his bear ship during the past four or five years has been immense, for with age also came wisdom and a sweet tooth, and during late years, a fresh bullock, calf, or a pig, has invariably been required at each and every repast in order to satisfy his appetite. In his early days, it is said, he not unfrequently returned to the dead carcass of a slaughtered bullock, for a second and even third repast, but age and experience had probably taught him the necessity of abandoning this practice, lest he in turn should fall a victim. Numerous and energetic have been the efforts made by the people of Marin to destroy this monster.” The Big Bear of Marin Killed, Hydraulic Press, July 7 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for GB.1860.7 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “We learn from the Santa Cruz Sentinel that, as the Rev. Mr. West, who lives near Scott's ranch, was returning to his home, he came suddenly upon a large grizzly bear, which was partially hidden by the undergrowth. His bearship, with a growl, made at the Parson, who, in turn, took to his heels. The race was long and exciting; the Parson was in good running trim, and being somewhat frightened, made good time. He solemnly declares to a friend that, for two hundred yards, he thinks he made the best time on record.” A Preacher Running For His Life, Sacramento Daily Union, July 6 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1860.3 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “The wild animals must be rather dangerous about Santa Cruz. Not long since, the Rev. Mr. West was chased by a grizzly, but being in good running condition, and somewhat frightened, he succeeded in keeping himself out of bruin’s grasp. Mr. Asa Ellis, who lives in the neighborhood had a valuable horse killed week before last by a California lion.” Varmints, Nevada Democrat, July 11 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.ML.1860.1 and T.GB.1860.3 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “I consider it my most painful duty to inform you and your numerous readers of the melancholy death of a young man, whose name is Barkley Woodward, a native of the State of Pennsylvania and aged about twenty-six years. He came to his death by being torn by a bear. I will relate to you the circumstances of his death. It was on Monday, the 25th of June, that the man left the place where he was encamped to go in search of some cattle which appeared to be missing.” Fatal Fight With A Grizzly, Sacramento Daily Union, July 13 1860. Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1860.4 for full context and citations. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection.

descriptions.value: “A grizzly bear, which has infested Stanislaus county recently, has made way with some ten or fifteen hogs, belonging to Lord & Thomas.” A Hard Customer, May 17 1860, Sacramento Daily Union. The Daily National Democrat published on 18 May: “A correspondent of the Stockton Argus, writing from Upper San Joaquin, says: “A grizzly bear has infested his neighborhood for some time past, and has been doing a general butchering business among the hogs. During the week he has killed some ten or fifteen hogs belonging to Lord & Thomas. — Judging from the size of his foot (which measures thirteen inches from heel to toe,) he must be one of the “oldest inhabitants.”” Please see the Predator Microhistory Network's entry for T.GB.1860.1 for full context and citations. Pending further context this microhistory's location has been set to a location central to Stockton, California. This microhistory can be accessed through the California Digital Newspaper Collection (CDNC).
